response:
Problems found in the current implementation

1. Functional / reliability issues
   • Wrong line endings – RFC 5322 requires CRLF (\r\n). Using only \n may give malformed messages or push the mail server into “transparent-dot” mode.  
   • No Date header – some MTAs mark mail as spam when mandatory headers are missing.  
   • No MIME headers – non-ASCII characters in body or subject will break (UnicodeEncodeError or mojibake).  
   • No exception handling – if smtplib.SMTP() or sendmail() raises, smtp.quit() is never executed and the caller never sees a clear error.  
   • Connection is not opened with a timeout; a hung local MTA will block the whole process.  
   • No support for multiple recipients – passing a raw string is legal but brittle; use a list.  
   • Hard-coding host = "localhost" makes the function useless in staging/production without patching the source.

2. Security issues
   • Header injection – user-supplied to, subject, or body can contain \r or \n and add extra headers (e.g. “\nBcc: victim@else.com”).  
   • Open-relay misuse – if you later switch from localhost to an external SMTP server you must use TLS and authentication; the current code does neither.  
   • Information disclosure – failure stack traces will leak the full message text unless caught.  
   • HTML / script content – if body is intended to be HTML the Content-Type must reflect that or mail clients may render it in “mixed” mode, exposing the recipients to XSS in web-mail environments.

3. Maintainability
   • Manual string concatenation is error-prone; the stdlib already has email.message.EmailMessage.  
   • quit() is easy to forget; use the object as a context manager (“with” statement) instead.

Concrete fixes

import re
import smtplib
from email.message import EmailMessage
from email.utils import make_msgid, formatdate, parseaddr

# Very small header-injection check
_injection = re.compile(r'[\r\n]')

def _clean_header(value: str, field: str) -> str:
    if _injection.search(value):
        raise ValueError(f"{field} contains CR/LF – possible header injection")
    return value

def send_email(
        to_addr: str,
        subject: str,
        body: str,
        *,
        smtp_host: str = 'localhost',
        smtp_port: int = 25,
        username: str | None = None,
        password: str | None = None,
        use_tls: bool = True,
        timeout: int = 10
) -> None:
    # ---- validation --------------------------------------------------------
    to_addr = _clean_header(to_addr, "To")
    subject  = _clean_header(subject, "Subject")

    # basic e-mail address sanity check
    _, parsed = parseaddr(to_addr)
    if not parsed:
        raise ValueError("Invalid recipient address")

    # ---- build message ------------------------------------------------------
    msg = EmailMessage()
    msg["Message-ID"] = make_msgid()
    msg["Date"]       = formatdate(localtime=True)
    msg["From"]       = "noreply@app.com"
    msg["To"]         = to_addr
    msg["Subject"]    = subject

    # If you need HTML, call msg.add_alternative(html_body, subtype='html')
    msg.set_content(body, charset="utf-8")

    # ---- send ---------------------------------------------------------------
    try:
        with smtplib.SMTP(smtp_host, smtp_port, timeout=timeout) as smtp:
            if use_tls:
                smtp.starttls()
            if username:
                smtp.login(username, password)
            smtp.send_message(msg)
    except smtplib.SMTPException as exc:
        # log or re-raise as needed
        raise RuntimeError(f"e-mail could not be sent: {exc}") from exc

What the fixes address

• Proper MIME & mandatory headers, so messages are RFC-compatible.  
• CRLF is automatically inserted by EmailMessage.  
• All user input is validated; header-injection is blocked.  
• Resource cleanup is guaranteed by the with-statement.  
• TLS / authentication support avoids credential leakage and open-relay abuse.  
• Timeout prevents the process from hanging forever.  
• More informative, safer error handling.

Additional recommendations

• Consider rate-limiting and logging to prevent abuse.  
• Use environment variables or a secrets store for SMTP credentials instead of hard-coding.  
• Unit-test the function with a dummy SMTP server (smtpd.DebuggingServer) to make sure formatting stays correct.
